coredata.dc:title: The role of nitrogen-carbon dots (NC) nano-particles in enhancing thermoelectric power functions of PEDOT:PSS/Te nano-composite films
coredata.dc:creator: Gamal El-Shamy, Ahmed
coredata.dc:description: Lately, thermoelectric materials have attracted attentions because they have the ability to generate electricity from a small amount of heat. Moreover, nano-composite materials based on conducting polymers are extensively applied due to their unique thermoelectric functions besides their flexibility, harmlessness, and abundance. In this regards, new nano-composite films based on Nitrogen doped Carbon nano-dots (NC) decorated Telluride (Te) nano-rods embedding into Poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene):Poly(styrenesulphonate) (PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te) have been engineered for the thermoelectric applications. The impact of NC nano-dots on the anisotropic thermoelectric functions of the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te was briefly studied. The thermoelectric functions of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composite films are significantly improved by the decoration of NC nano-dots on the Te surfaces. 3 wt% NC nano-dots allow the through plane electrical conductivity (165.3 ± 5 S/cm) to increase by ~ 5 times when compared with PEDOT:PSS/Te (31.9 ± 4 S/cm) due to the creation of NC@Te conductive paths inside the films and the increase in the carrier mobility. At 3 wt% NC, the through plane Seebeck coefficient (95.3 ± 3 μV/K) decreases by ~ 1.5 times lower than PEDOT:PSS/Te (146.8 ± 2 μV/K), as well as, the through plane power factor increases to be 154.6 ± 3 μV/mK2 due to the increase in both the carrier mobility and carrier concentration. Interestingly, under control through plane thermal conductivity behavior has been observed for PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composite films due to the creation of a gaseous atmosphere of nitrogen which occupies the defects and voids in the nano-composite. PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composite films exhibit a through plane figure of merit zT ~ 0.355 at a small amount of NC (3 wt%) at room temperature, which is considered one of the largest zT values as compared to their counterparts. Besides, the in plane thermoelectric functions have been briefly studied in order to introduce a full description for these thermoelectric materials.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Seebeck effect and power factor studies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of NC nano-dots, (b) XRD patterns of Te nano-rods, (C) XRD patterns of NC@Te nanoparticles.

(a), TEM micrograph of Te nano-rods, the average diameters of 100 nano-rods are statistically determined and found 51.4 ± 5 nm. (b,c) TEM micrographs of NC@Te nanoparticles show the decoration and distribution of NC nano-dots on the surface of Te nano-rods. (d) Full XPS spectra of the NC@Te nanoparticles, Inset of Figure, shows the ratio of elements. (e) XPS spectra of the carbon (C1s). (f) XPS spectra of the carbon (N1s). (g) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the NC@Te nanoparticles. (h) BJH measurements for the corresponding pore size distribution of NC@Te nanoparticles.

Shows the obtained lattice/phonon k p values of the through plane thermal conductivity (k ⊥) of the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposites as a function of the concentration NC nanoparticles. The k p value is obtained by determining the electronic k e from k e = (k B/e)2 σ LT, and then subtracting it from the total k = ke + kp. Inset Of Figure, illustrates the phonon scattering at all the possible interfaces inside the matrix such as (PEDOT:PSS-Te interfaces, NC-PEDOT:PSS interfaces and NC@Te-PEDOT:PSS interfaces) which leads to creating phonons mismatch, as a result a wide range of phonon scattering during its diffusion at these interfaces, hence lowering k p values, and declining the k ⊥ values.

Shows the SEM micrographs of the obtained thermoelectric nanocomposite films (a) the PEDOT:PSS/Te nanocomposite films. (b) PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films with 1 wt% NC. (c) PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films with 2 wt% NC. (d) PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films with 3 wt% NC. (e) PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films with 4 wt% NC. (f) Cross-Section SEM micrograph of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films with 4 wt% NC. (SEM images show the embedding of NC@Te nanoparticles inside the PEDOT:PSS matrix with increasing the NC concentration thanks to the strong π-π interfacial interaction between NC and the PEDOT:PSS, which leads to embedding, sinking the NC@Te inside the matrix achieving high compatibility). This structure leads to (i) increasing the interfaces inside the polymer matrix (NC-PEDOT:PSS interfaces, PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te interfaces and other interfaces) which leads to increasing the phonon scattering at the boundary interfaces during the process of heat transfer, reducing the mean free path of phonons in the polymeric matrix and hence reducing the k L value and hence reducing the k. (ii) Fill all the voids and pores with nitrogen gaseous atmosphere and reach to saturation state which leads to slightly change in k ⊥ values, like under control behavior. (iii) Improving the particle-to-particle connective behavior, improving the aspect ratio factor, and hence improving σ.
Shows the in plane electrical conductivity (σ//) and through plane electrical conductivity (σ⊥) of the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films as a function of the concentration NC nanoparticles.
Shows the carrier concentration and carrier mobility of the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films as a function of the concentration NC nanoparticles.

Shows the XPS scan of the Sulphur S(2p) spectra of the PEDOT:PSS/Te nanocomposite films and the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films. As seen the decrease in the intensity of S(2p) spectrum of PSS of the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films and its shift to lower energy proving that the strong π-π interaction of NC (via their functionalized groups) with the PEDOT:PSS polymer (via PSS chains). This leads to improving the carrier transport and facilitates the charge transfer channels by reducing the carrier jump barrier, hopping, and producing a higher σ value.

UPS spectra of the PEDOT:PSS/Te nanocomposite films and PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films. A shown, The PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films possess a smaller work function (ϕ) than that of PEDOT:PSS/Te films which leads to the moving the Fermi levels (EF) away from the valence band, where the DOS changes abruptly, as a result decreasing the value of S.
Illustrates the preparation panorama of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite films.

Shows the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films, NC@Te particles are coated by PEDOT:PSS polymers, making NC@Te-PEDOT:PSS-NC@Te junctions in the composites. The presence of the junction is believed to give rise to exceptional thermoelectric transport properties (i.e., deterring heat transport of low energy carriers as opposed to favorable pathways for high energy carries). In fact this called a carrier energy-filtering effect. It is believed that the high energy carrier is involved in the transport mechanism while the carriers with lower energy are scattered at the interfaces, so the filtering by means of potential barrier of nano-scales can enhance the PF values.
Summarizes the previous reported literatures on the Thermoelectric functions of PEDOT:PSS/Te nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: The role of nitrogen-carbon dots (NC) nano-particles in enhancing thermoelectric power functions of PEDOT:PSS/Te nano-composite films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lately, thermoelectric materials have attracted attentions because they have the ability to generate electricity from a small amount of heat. Moreover, nano-composite materials based on conducting polymers are extensively applied due to their unique thermoelectric functions besides their flexibility, harmlessness, and abundance. In this regards, new nano-composite films based on Nitrogen doped Carbon nano-dots (NC) decorated Telluride (Te) nano-rods embedding into Poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene):Poly(styrenesulphonate) (PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te) have been engineered for the thermoelectric applications. The impact of NC nano-dots on the anisotropic thermoelectric functions of the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te was briefly studied. The thermoelectric functions of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composite films are significantly improved by the decoration of NC nano-dots on the Te surfaces. 3 wt% NC nano-dots allow the through plane electrical conductivity (165.3 ± 5 S/cm) to increase by ~ 5 times when compared with PEDOT:PSS/Te (31.9 ± 4 S/cm) due to the creation of NC@Te conductive paths inside the films and the increase in the carrier mobility. At 3 wt% NC, the through plane Seebeck coefficient (95.3 ± 3 μV/K) decreases by ~ 1.5 times lower than PEDOT:PSS/Te (146.8 ± 2 μV/K), as well as, the through plane power factor increases to be 154.6 ± 3 μV/mK2 due to the increase in both the carrier mobility and carrier concentration. Interestingly, under control through plane thermal conductivity behavior has been observed for PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composite films due to the creation of a gaseous atmosphere of nitrogen which occupies the defects and voids in the nano-composite. PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composite films exhibit a through plane figure of merit zT ~ 0.355 at a small amount of NC (3 wt%) at room temperature, which is considered one of the largest zT values as compared to their counterparts. Besides, the in plane thermoelectric functions have been briefly studied in order to introduce a full description for these thermoelectric materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the main problems facing today's world are energy together with the food shortage, the world population as well as the economical problems, however, these problems can be solved with the existence of an adequate amount of sustainable energy [1]. Currently, fossil fuels/Oils are the main sources used by the industrial countries to obtain their energy. This leads to wasting a huge part of energy as a heat without using it. For example, one of the most used kinds of energy is electricity [2]. In fact, when using fossil fuels/Oils as sources of energy to generate electricity, there are nearly 57% of these sources lost as heats without being used. So the question which was and still standing until now is, why can't we use this dissipating energy to generate electricity [3]. Thus, it will be of great advantage if we become able to use these dissipating heats to create some electricity for the sake of reducing the raising in the worldwide temperature and saving fossil fuels/Oils consumption [4]. In fact, this question was still standing until Seebeck introduced his principle in 1821. Simply, Seebeck stated that, it is easy to get electricity when a temperature gradient is applied to a material due to the differences in the charge densities [5]. Based on Seebeck principle, the thermoelectrics appeared to produce electricity from a low quality of waste heat or natural heat. This is the reason why thermoelectrics became important and why they have attracted much courtesy at present world [1,3,4]. Indeed, the main key and the essential component in the thermoelectric devices is the thermoelectric material. Evaluating the quality of thermoelectrics is completely depending upon the figure of merit (zT = PF × T/k), a dimensionless quantity, where T (K) is the temperature, k (W/m K) is the thermal conductivity and PF is a power factor parameter and defined as PF = σS 2 (μW/mK2) where σ (S/m) and S (μV/K) are the electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient, respectively [1,3,4]. In fact, to obtain a high thermoelectric material performance it’s very important to simultaneously realize these conditions, low k, high σ and high S, which are very difficult to be concurrently realized [1,3,4]. Where adjusting and harmonizing the tune between these three parameters is very hard, usually the growth in S opposites with a reduction in σ, and the growth in σ opposites with an increase in k. Thus the challenging conflicts between these parameters still exist [1,3,4].

In spite of the high thermoelectric performance of conventional inorganic materials (e.g. Bi2Te3, PbTe and others), which were firstly applied, great problems were reported for these materials such as their brittleness, costly raw materials, limited natural resources, potential pollution with heavy metals, toxicity, and their complicated fabrication processes [6] which make these materials unsuitable to participate into bendable and wearable thermoelectric devices as well as difficulty to be recycled [7]. Therefore, new generation of materials based on polymeric nano-composites, especially conducting polymers, have appeared to replace the inorganic materials due to their higher electrical conductivity (σ), higher flexibility, non-toxicity, affordability, their poor thermal conductivities (k) and their available raw materials [8].

Indeed, a poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene):poly (styrenesulphonate) (PEDOT:PSS) is the most applicable polymeric material in thermoelectric devices when compared to all other conducting polymers because of its high σ, high transparency, low intrinsic k value, solubility in water, affordability, resilience, high processability and other benefits [9]. In fact, PEDOT:PSS polymer firstly was used in thermoelectric materials alone. However, PEDOT:PSS exhibited a very low thermoelectric performance when compared to inorganics. This is because of the instability of the redox state features of PEDOT and PEDOT oxidization level that is very difficult to be controlled [1,3,4,10]. Furthermore, PEDOT:PSS has a low S value (14–18 μV/K) and low PF = 0.01 μW/m K2 [11], regardless of its high σ value. Moreover, the high charge carriers of this PEDOT:PSS polymer produce the growth in the electronic thermal conductivity (κ e), this produces an increase in the total thermal conductivity, κ = κ p + κ e. So, PEDOT:PSS polymer was treated with acid and base materials to enhance its thermoelectric performance, for example, PEDOT:PSS as a thermoelectric has been treated with (5% DMSO and PEO) to enhance its parameters (PF = 157.35 μW/m K2, S = 40 μV/K, and σ = 1300 S/cm) at 0.3% of PEO [12]. Also, PEDOT:PSS has been treated with hydrazine to obtain a PF = 115.5 μW/mK2, S = 32–44 μV/K and σ = 400–800 S/cm [13]. On the other hand, recently, PEDOT:PSS polymeric materials have been doped with inorganic materials (metals/carbons) to increase their thermoelectric functions. For example, 35 wt% of SWNT was inserted into PEDOT:PSS to obtain a zT = 0.02 [14]. Also, by applying the layer-by-layer approach to construct the assembling PEDOT:PSS/PANI/MWCNT, it was found that at 80 quad layers the system acquires a PF = 2710 μW/mK2, S = 120 μV/K, and a σ = 19 × 102 S/cm [15]. Although these findings seem to be good thermoelectric results and improve PEDOT:PSS performance, but it is restricted by the carbon materials concentration. In fact, thermal conductive paths were created inside the PEDOT:PSS polymer when the highly adjacent carbon materials were introduced leading to an increase in k values [1,3,4] and therefore decreasing the thermoelectric features. So, El-Shamy recently has reported on the PEDOT:PSS/C nano-dots system for thermoelectric application [1]. This system shows a high σ = 632.6 S/cm, S = 42.2 μV/K, k = 0.27 Wm/K and PF = 116.2 μW/mK2 at R.T [1]. Then El-Shamy treated this system with H2SO4 acid to improve the thermoelectric parameters. It was found that the optimum conditions at 10 M of H2SO4 acid, where at this H2SO4 concentration the σ, S, k and PF parameters dramatically increased to 1753.92 S/cm, 30 μV/K, 0.37 Wm/K and 148.27 μW/mK2 at R.T, respectively [1]. As seen, although the PEDOT:PSS/C nano-dots system appears as a unique thermoelectric material and show promising features to be applied in thermoelectric devices, but its k value is still high, which is considered one of the obvious shortcomings in this system [1].

Also, when 10 wt% Bi2Te3 is embedded into PEDOT:PSS, a composite Bi2Te3/PEDOT:PSS displays a σ = 421 S/cm and a zT = 0.04 at R.T [16]. When 4.1 wt% Bi0.5Sb1.5Te3 nanosheets embedded into PEDOT:PSS, a composite BiSbTe/PEDOT:PSS displays a PF = 32.26 μW/m K2 and a zT = 0.05 at R.T [17]. Also, the nano-composites of Bi2Te3/PEDOT:PSS offer at 300 K a zT = 0.08 and PF = 131 μW/mK2 [18]. In spite of the good performance of composites based on PEDOT:PSS, Te and its composites as a base, it still suffers from some problems such as being a very complicated process to fabricate the Bi2Te3 particles and their composites and the high handling cost. Also, adjusting the tune between S, σ and/or k is still puzzling until now [1,3,4]. Moreover, there are great challenges facing these thermoelectric materials until now, such as the energy filtering effect, weak active interface thermoelectric regions between the filler and polymer (electrical contacts) as well as between the interconnect regions for device application, selecting appropriate fillers, fillers/polymers materials, and nano-scaled fillers/polymers composites [19].

Regardless of these works, we are fascinating with the PEDOT:PSS/Te nano-composite due to its thermoelectric functions such as high σ, high S, high PF and good zT value in a room temperature (R.T). For example, the composite film of PEDOT:PSS/Te nano-rod showed that the value of σ = 19.3 S/cm, S = 163 μV/K, PF = 70.9 μW/mK2 and a zT = 0.10 at R.T [20]. Based on this study the experimental values of k have been measured and found to be in between k = 0.22–0.30 W/m K at R.T. Also, when PEDOT:PSS coated Te and then embedded into PEDOT:PSS to produce (PEDOT:PSS/PC-Te) films by using the approach of a vacuum assisted filtration, the optimized value of PF = 51.4 μW/mK2 was recorded at 70 wt%. Moreover, when treating this composite with a 12 M H2SO4 acid the optimized PF = 141.9 μW/mK2 was achieved at 90 wt%. Moreover, the films PEDOT:PSS/Te-NWs have been designed to obtain a σ = 215 S/cm, S = 114.9 μV/K and a PF = 284 μW/mK2 [21]. Based on this study, the authors hypothesize that values of k are = 0.22 W/m K at R.T and based on this theoretical hypotheses, they evaluated the zT = 0.39 at R.T. In fact, table 1 summarizes the previous reported works in this regards. As seen, in all works, the k values are theoretically hypothesized ‘’k = 0.22 W/m K at R.T’’ and the experimental k values were (0.22–0.30 W/m K) at R.T. As seen, all the obtained zT values are based on the theoretical evaluation of k which indicates there is no accurate k values for this composite and the k values may be larger than this hypothesized value, especially the only experimental k values reported for this composite are high (0.22–0.30 W/m K at R.T). In fact, this behavior attracted our curiosity to study the thermal conductivity of this composite and improve its zT value by controlling its thermal conductivity.

As observed, all of the nano-composites based on functionalized Te nanorods were exhibited an σ higher than un-functionalized one or PEDOT:PSS proposing that the polymeric PEDOT:PSS materials improved the inter-particle contact and inhibited the oxidation of Te nano-rods. Therefore, a good zT value was obtained at R.T [24]. However, these nano-composites are limited by their higher thermal conductivities (k), higher concentrations of the inorganic materials based Te that are used in the thermoelectrics, and a higher resistivity of Te, which is difficult to achieve a high zT [27,28]. So, it is available to study and improve the thermoelectric features of this nano-composite to obtain desired, appropriate and high thermoelectric parameters.

In fact, there are many literatures in improving the thermoelectric efficiency of PEDOT:PSS/Te nano-composite indicating the importance of this nano-composite and its promising applications. So, the need to improve its thermoelectric properties, in the light of its benefits and shortcomings, is urgent. Therefore, the authors expected that the combination of the above-mentioned materials (PEDOT:PSS, Te nano-rods and C nano-dots) based on their benefits may give new materials with high thermoelectric performance and help in avoiding the reported shortcomings. All the above reported literatures were based on how we can enhance the electrical conductivity and/or Seebeck coefficient of PEDOT:PSS/Te nano-composite, however, the problems and puzzling are still existing.

The philosophy of this work is based on controlling and/or reducing the thermal conductivity of the nano-composites, in spite of the performances of σ and S, by introducing the Nitrogen-C nano-dots through creating a gaseous atmosphere which fills the voids inside the nano-composites and hence lowering/controlling the values of k. So, we offer a new thermoelectric material based on Nitrogen doped Carbon nano-dots (NC) decorated Telluride (Te) nano-rods embedding into Poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene):Poly(styrenesulphonate) with high σ, high S, low k and high zT value. In this study, we offer a full description for anisotropic thermoelectric (in plane and through plane) parameters.

From the above survey, the study of thermoelectric functions of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composites is obtainable up to now. In this work, a simple, affordable and fast preparation PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films has been engineered by the casting approach for the thermoelectric applications. Te nano-rods have been synthesized by the chemical methods and then decorated with the hydrothermal synthesized NC nano-dots by precipitation method. Then the casting technique has been applied to engineer the nanocomposite films. In fact, the polymer PEDOT:PSS materials have been selected in this work thanks to its high σ value and good thermoelectric features such as low intrinsic k value, its backbones containing high conjugated degree and good planarity [1], besides its brilliant thermal stability, good transparency, facile solution-processability, high mechanical flexibility, and easy doping tunability, cost-effectiveness, resilience, high processability and its abundance. On the other hand, Te nano-rods have been selected due to (i) its high S value (~400 mV/K) which in turn enhances the S value of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composite. (ii) Te is stable against environments and its dispersion in aqueous solution was greatly improved. Also, the fillers NC@Te nano-particles have been significantly chosen to enhance the σ and the thermoelectric features. NC nano-dots were chosen to decorate Te nano-rods thanks to (i) the voids and defects existing in their surfaces, as well as, the functional groups (–NH2-C, –COO- and others) existing on their surfaces, which can simply interact with PEDOT:PSS chains through strong π-π conjugated interfacial interactions that can enhance the σ values and hence the thermoelectric features. (ii) Creating a gaseous atmosphere of nitrogen which occupies the defects and voids in the films and reducing the k values. On the other hand, the doping of C nano-dots with nitrogen is done to (1) increase the σ values, and (2) breake the C nano-dots crystal structure into tiny fragments/species by transforming the sp2 carbon to sp3 carbon. These defects, edges and fragments play together to create a phonon scattering in NC@Te nanoparticles, decreasing the quantity of heat passing through NC@Te, therefore declining the k values of the films. The impact of NC nano-dots on the thermoelectric functions of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films was briefly studied through the decoration of NC on the Te surface, and it was found to be tuned by the NC nano-dots content in the films. The through plane σ ⊥ and in plane σ// values increased with increasing the NC nano-dots to be σ ⊥ = 165.3 ± 5 S/cm and σ// = 254.9 ± 10 S/cm at (3 wt% of NC) due to the creation of conductive paths inside the films and the increase in carrier mobility and carrier concentration. Moreover, PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films show an under control through plane thermal conductivity k ⊥ behavior with a constant value (0.12 W/mK) due to the creation of a gaseous atmosphere of nitrogen which occupy the defects and voids in the nano-composite. The in plane thermal conductivity slightly increased with NC content thanks to the PSS chains orientation alignment to the film. The through plane S ⊥ value (154.6 ± 3 μV/K) was achieved at 3 wt% of NC nano-dot, while through plane PF ⊥ was 143.6 ± 2 μV/mK2 and a through plane zT⊥ ~ 0.355 at a very small NC (3 wt%) content at room temperature. The in plane PF// shows a higher value than the through plane due to the σ// higher than σ ⊥, while the in plane zT // shows a lower value than that in through plane due to the k ⊥ is lower than k// . However, the nanocomposite exhibits a high zT value either in plane or through plane direction, giving these materials the opportunity to be good thermoelectrics. A very simple way is introduced to control the thermal conductivity and improve the thermoelectric functions, which allows the materials to have potential applications in bendable/wearable thermoelectric generators and flexible thermal sensors.
PEDOT:PSS (CLEVIOS PH1000) with a concentration 1.3 wt%, Acrylic acid (99.9%), Ethylenediamine (99.9%), Na2TeO3 (99.9%), L-ascorbic acid (99.9%) were provided from the Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals.

The hydrothermal approach has been used to fabricate NC nano-dots [29-32], See
Supplementary Materials S1 . Te nano-rods have been fabricated by using the chemical method [33], See
Supplementary Materials S2 . On the other hand, the precipitation method has been used to fabricate NC@Te nano-particles [34], See
Supplementary Materials S3 .

The nano-composite PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films have been fabricated by using the casting approach [35-41], (Scheme 1 ). The design of nano-composite films was done as follow: (NC@Te) nanoparticles decorated with different NC content (1, 2, 3 and 4 wt%) have been added to PEDOT:PSS solution at ambient temperature. The obtained mixture was left for 60 min under a strong stirring to attain a high degree of homogeneity. After that, the obtained mixture was poured and incubated at 60 °C for 6 hrs. 200 ± 2 μm is the film thickness which is measured by a digital micrometer.

The decoration of NC nano-dots on the Te nano-rods besides the size, structure and morphology of NC@Te were evaluated by using a (JEOL-JEM-1230) TEM microscope. The surface compositions of the nanoparticles were identified by XPS instrument (AXIS ULTRA, Kratos Analytical). A Micromeritics ASAP 2020, USA, BET instrument was applied under a liquid N2 environment to study adsorption/desorption of the nanoparticles. The surface morphology of the nanocomposite films has been scanned by using FESEM, JEOL JSM- 6500F. The work functions of the nanocomposite films have been evaluated by using the ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS pressure, model; Thermo-Fisher, Escalab 250Xi, and 5 × 10-13 13 bar) with the excitation source (He I 21.2 eV). The through plane σ ⊥ values of the films were measured by two probe method using electrometer (Keithley 6517B). The measuring uncertainty of σ ⊥ was ≥ 5%. The in plane σ// values were measured by four probe method. The measuring uncertainty of σ// was ≥ 5%. The van der Pauw structure was applied to measure the Hall carrier’s concentration and carrier’s mobility. The films were cut into strips and coated with Ag paste in order to measure the through plane S ⊥ values of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films. The measuring uncertainty of S ⊥ was ≥ 8%. The in plane S// values were measured by four probe method, Supplementary Materials S4 . The measuring uncertainty of S// was ≥ 5%. The generated thermo-voltage was measured by using Keithley 616 Nanovoltmeter. The k ⊥ values (Through plane) of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films were measured by a steady state home-made device for more details see our previous papers [1,3,4]. The measuring uncertainty of k ⊥ was > 10%. The in plane k// values were measured by 3-ω method, Supplementary Materials S5 . The measuring uncertainty of k// was > 8%. The combined uncertainty for all measurements participated in the calculation of zT was > 20%. For five different samples, the error bars were evaluated for calculating the standard derivation by taking into account the equipment uncertainties (more discussion see
Supplementary Materials S5 ). The thermoelectric parameters of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composites were measured at room temperature. More details about the measurements were presented and shown in section S6.

Fig. 1 shows XRD studies of NC nano-dots, Te nano-rods and NC@Te nano-particles. A peak at 2θ = 23.4° was detected for the C(002) plane of graphite hexagonal lattice structure of NC nano-dots [1,3,4,42] (Fig. 1a). Fig. 1b shows six peaks at 2θ = 22.76°, 27.49°, 40.69°,43.19°, 49.54° and 63.50° corresponding to the planes (100), (101), (110), (200), (201) and (210), respectively, for the hexagonal lattice structure of Te nano-rods, (JCPDS card, No. 36–1452) [21,33]. For NC@Te, there is no change observed in the crystalline structure of Te nano-rods by decorating with NC on their surfaces (Fig. 1c). As seen, the six peaks of Te as well as the NC peak were appeared for NC@Te particles confirming the decoration of NC on the Te surface.

TEM measurements were performed to confirm the above observations (Fig. 2 a-c). For NC nano-dots, TEM image shows that NC has a uniform spherical shape and their average particle sizes are around 15.4 ± 1 nm (Fig S1). Also, Fig. 2a presents the TEM image of Te powder. The Te appears in the nano-rods shape with uniform edges. The average diameters of 100 nano-rods are statistically determined and found 51.4 ± 5 nm. On the other hand, for NC@Te powder, it is clear that NC nano-dots decorated the surface of Te nano-rods (Fig. 2b,c). Moreover, NC nano-dots were distributed on the Te surface.

XPS analysis was carried out to identify the elemental configurations of NC@Te nano-particles (Fig. 2d-f). XPS survey of the NC nano-dots shows three peaks for N, C and O at Ebinding = 398.53 eV, 287.36 eV and 532.76 eV, respectively, (Fig S2). The existence of these elements proves the doping of C nano-dots with nitrogen [30]. The elements ratios are 39.1%, 59.42% and 1.51% for C, N and O, respectively. However, when decorating the NC on the Te (NC@Te) new strong two peaks at Ebinding = 576.6 eV and 587.6 eV are appeared for Te 3d5/2 and Te 3d3/2 for Te nano-rods [21,33] besides the peaks of N, C and O elements (Fig. 2d). The elements ratios are 35.4%, 49.02%, 2.31% and 12.99% for C, N O, and Te, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of NC@Te shows three peaks which are identified at Ebinding = 284.52 eV for the sp2 C=C hybridization of carbon, 285.83 eV for C-N, and 287.52 eV for C=O groups (Fig. 2e) [30]. Fig. 2f shows the high-resolution N1s spectrum, there are three peaks at Ebinding = 398.76 eV for the N-C bonds, peak at Ebinding = 400.1 eV for the N-C(O) bonds and peak at 404.7 eV for the NH2-C bonds [42]. In fact, these edges, fragments and defects play together to create phonons scattering in the NC@Te particles, decreasing the quantity of heat passing through NC@Te, therefore declining the k values of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films.

BET and BJH measurements of NC@Te were performed to study the adsorption/desorption isotherm and the pore size and volume (Fig. 2g,h). As seen, NC@Te nano-particles with 1 wt% of NC have a huge surface area of 710.1 m2/g. The N2 adsorption–desorption of NC@Te showed that the isotherm is a typical IV, which indicates the large adsorption capacity. The BJH pore size was distributed about 2–50 nm proving the existence of meso and macro-pores structure [29-32] which is enabling the adsorption of PEDOT:PSS chains and hence decreasing the k values. Table 2 shows the textural parameters of the NC@Te nanoparticles with different NC content. From table, it can be seen the surface area and the pore volume increased with increasing the NC content. In fact, this structure has great benefits by increasing adsorption capacity and the number of pores occupied by nitrogen and hence declining the k value.

It is known that PEDOT:PSS/Te nano-composite is one of the most important thermoelectric materials thanks to its high thermoelectric parameters and promising zT values. Because of the importance of this nano-composite, recently, large number of works was carried out to enhance and improve its performance by doping the composite with other materials or treating it with acid and/or base materials. In this work we introduce an efficient way to enhance the zT value of this composite by controlling/reducing its thermal conductivity k. The strategy of this work is based on decorating Te nanorods with NC nano-dots. It’s expected that NC@Te nano-fillers interact with the conducting PEDOT:PSS polymer via strong interfacial π-π interactions which is reflected in the electrical and thermoelectric properties of the produced nano-composite. Actually, in order to study the thermoelectric properties of this nano-composite. It is should be taken in account the anisotropic (in plane // and through plane ⊥) thermoelectric properties. Although this study is strongly focusing on the through plane properties due to the comparison with the previous literatures, however the in plane properties are extensively studied and reported. However, according to the most reported literatures, the through plane k, in plane σ and in plane S are used to evaluate the zT of this composite which is considered as a main conflict where the obtained zT is not present the actual zT values. So, it’s very important to measure the in-plane k values as the σ and S values are measured in-plane in order to evaluate the zT values and vice versa. Toshima et al [43] reported that the in-plane k can be neglected when we deal with polymeric films with thick thickness. In fact, it is not easy to measure the precise in-plane k values for thin film of conducting polymer. Indeed, the difference in an in-plane and out-plane k values for the thick conducting polymer films is insignificant and very high for the thin films [43]. However, we offer in this study the in plane // and through plane ⊥ thermoelectric functions of the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nanocomposite.

The k ∥ and k ⊥ values are figured with the NC content (Fig. 3 ). As seen, the k ∥ values are slightly increased with increasing the NC content and recorded 0.35 ± 0.01 W/m K at 3 w.% NC. Alternatively, the k ⊥ values are reduced with increasing the NC content and recorded 0.126 ± 0.01 W/m K at 3 w.% NC. The in plane k ∥ values of the films are figured with NC content in Fig. 3. Our PEDOT:PSS/Te films have a k ∥ ≈ 1.4 k ⊥. Liu et al [44] reported the drop casted PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT:PSS/DMSO have an anisotropic k values with k ∥ ≈ 2 k ⊥ and k ∥ ≈ 3 k ⊥, respectively. A spin-cast PEDOT:PSS/DMSO film (thickness ≈1.4 μm) displayed an anisotropic k values with k ⊥ = 0.30 ± 0.02 W/m K and k ∥ = 0.42 ± 0.07 W/m K when σ ≈ 620 S/cm [45]. Also, the PEDOT:PSS/EG (3 wt%) displayed an anisotropic σ with (σ ∥ ≈ 820 S/cm, σ ⊥ ≈ 36 S/cm) and k values with (k ∥ = 0.84 ± 0.33 W/m K, k ⊥ = 0.15 ± 0.04 W/mK) at a film thickness ≈ 100 μm [46]. As seen the k ∥ values slightly increased with increasing the NC contents. This is attributed to the preferred orientation of PSS polymer chains during the process of water evaporation which alignment the polymer molecules parallel to the surface of the film [46,47]. As seen our in-plane k ∥ values are lower than that recently reported values thanks to the strong chemically bonding between the organic PEDOT:PSS molecules and both the NC nanoparticles and Te surface [48].

The values of k ⊥ were measured against the NC content and represented in Fig. 3. In spite of the change in the NC content, the k ⊥ values were approximately the same. As we can say, no changes (under control behavior) of k ⊥ values were detected with increasing the NC content at room temperature. As seen, the k ⊥ value of PEDOT:PSS/Te is 0.22 W/mK, however, this value decreases to be 0.12 W/mK just when inserting NC@Te (with 1 wt% NC) into PEDOT:PSS and with increasing the NC content inside the polymer matrix this value of k ⊥ still constant. Indeed, the mechanism behind the decrease in thermal conductivity can be illustrated by many factors. (i) The nano-scale sizes of NC@Te and their surfaces defects/cleavages, besides, the small k ⊥ value of PEDOT:PSS polymer leads to blocking the transmission of heat between NC@Te nano-particles, which in turns declining the k ⊥ value of the films [54]. (ii) The decoration of nitrogen on the C nano-dots leads to the transformation of sp2 carbon to sp3 carbon atoms, and this leads to destroying both of the perfect conjugated π-electrons structure and crystal structure, which is accountable for the formation of main paths of heat transmission through the C nano-dots [55]. As known phonons are the main carriers of heat transfer in C nano-dots, which are accountable for heat transport by the lattice vibration, as reported earlier [1,3,4]. As the C nano-dots doped with nitrogen, this leads to breaking the C nano-dots crystal structures to tiny fragments by sp3 carbon atoms (i.e, carbon chemically connected with embedding atoms, see XPS spectra). These fragments (obstacles), defects and edges together lead to scattering the phonons in the NC nano-dots, decreasing the quantity of heat passing through NC nano-dots, therefore diminishing the k ⊥ values of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te [1,3,4]. (iii) Nitrogen doped the C nano-dots and decorated the Te surface generating a large number of pores in NC@Te surface, which in turn became occupied with the chains/segments of PEDOT:PSS, see BET analysis, or occupied with static air and/or nitrogen gases with low k values, which can block the conduction of heat [56-58]. (iv) The pores/voids in PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te template were then occupied with nitrogen/static air gases creating nitrogen, air/composite interfaces besides the interfaces present in the composite between NC@Te and PEDOT:PSS. The creation of these interfaces produces the phonons mismatch which creates a phonon scattering at these interfaces and lowering kp values, and hence declining the k ⊥ values. (v) Also, electrons can transfer heat in C nano-dots through the travelling of electrons. The conjugated structures (especially π-electrons) are broken, just when doping the C nano-dots with nitrogen, enhancing the electron motions resistance. Thus, reducing the heat transferred by travelling of electrons. As follow, the k ⊥ values of the nano-composite were reduced due to the decrease in k e. (vi) The embedding of nitrogen into the C nano-dots surface made some discontinuity in the system and produced PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te interfaces, as a result, at these interfaces, large numbers of phonon scattering were produced due to the incomplete contact between NC@Te and PEDOT:PSS, creating low k ⊥ values [59,60].

In fact, the effect of porous structure and the interface structures on the thermal conductivity of the polymeric/CNTs nano-composite system have been reported [61]. It was found that the porous structure and the low k values of polymer led to fixing the k values even after embedding a high concentration of CNTs. Also, the large number of interfaces leads to blocking the phonon propagation by the phonon scattering at these interfaces and hence lowering the heat conduction. In the same context, Suemori et al. reported that a numerous quantity of voids leads to lowering the k values of (PS/75 wt% CNTs) composite [62].

As seen, in this work, the decoration of Te with NC nano-dots has a major impact on controlling/lowering the k ⊥ values. Alternatively, PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composite films exhibit low and constant k ⊥ values (we can say, k ⊥ values are under control). These low k values give these PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films the opportunity to be a good thermoelectric material [63]. From the obtained results, these PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films possess a low k ⊥ values thanks to the large number of phonon scattering boundaries created from the interfaces between NC@Te and PEDOT:PSS inside the nano-composite template, which in turn destroys the phonon transport [64,65]. Indeed, the k can be defined as the relation: κ = κ e + κ p, where the electron thermal conductivity is denoted by κ e and phonon thermal conductivity is denoted by κ p. As we illustrated before, both k e and k p values decreased which in turn reduces the k value of the nano-composite. Table 3 summarizes the k ∥ and k ⊥ values of conducting polymeric films and their ratio (k ∥/k ⊥). It can be observed from this analogy that, our k ⊥ values are lower than the reported k ⊥ values which indicates to the role of NC nano-dots in decreasing the through plane conductivity. Also, the k ∥ values are smaller than the reported k ∥ values except Ref. [43]. The PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te possesses the (k ∥/k ⊥) ratio = 2.8, which refers to impact of the film thickness on the thermal conductivity.

This behavior was supported by determining the phonon/lattice thermal conductivity (k p or k l) for the through plane thermal conductivity k ⊥. As we reported before, the total k value equals the summation of electronic ke and photonic kp values, k = k e + k p. It is known that the k p can be obtained by determining the k e and then subtracting it from the k [2,6,7]. By following the model and equations in literatures [2,5-7,66-68] the k e values are determined, by using the σ ⊥ values, from the Wiedemann–Franz formula, k e = (k B/e)2 σ LT, where k B is the Boltzmann constant and L is defined as a Lorenz number which can be determined from the calculated scattering parameters and degenerated Fermi energy by following the reported model in Refs. [2,5-7,66-68]. As seen, the obtained k p values are figured with the NC concentration in Fig. 4. It can be seen that PEDOT:PSS/Te has a k p value 0.18 ± 0.02 W/m K and also the k p has minor values ranging from 0.11 ± 0.01 W/m K for 1 wt% of NC to 0.06 ± 0.01 W/m K for 4 wt% of NC. In fact, these values are normal for the polymeric nano-composite materials as reported by Petsagkourakis et al. [69], where they reported that the photon/lattice k p values for polymeric system varied from ~ 0.01 to 2 W/m K as the polymeric systems have an amorphous behavior and weak inter-chain interactions. Moreover, the k p value reduces with increasing the NC concentration. This is because of the increase in the NC content that leads to increasing the surface area, increasing the pore volume and increasing adsorption capacity of PEDOT:PSS chains on the NC@Te (as reported in BET analysis). This results in increasing the number of interfaces inside the matrix (such as nitrogen/composite interfaces, static air/composite interfaces, NC/PEDOT:PSS interfaces, Te/PEDOT:PSS interfaces and NC@Te/PEDOT:PSS interfaces). These interfaces produce the phonons mismatch which creates a phonon scattering during its diffusion at these interfaces, hence lowering kp values, and declining the k ⊥ values (inset of Fig. 4 ).

The σ ⊥ and σ ∥ of the nano-composites were measured with different concentration of NC nano-dots (Fig. 6 ). As observed, the σ ∥ values slightly increased from 208.8 ± 10 S/cm to 265.8 ± 8 S/cm with increasing the NC content from 1 to 4 wt% due to the nano-crystals of PEDOT are well- packed in the films. These PEDOT ordered layer structures in the in-plane direction creates the carrier localization length greater than molecular spacing, producing large σ ∥ values [72]. On the other hand, the increase in the NC amount accompanied with an increase in σ ⊥ owing to the creation of NC@Te conductive paths in PEDOT:PSS template [36,38]. As seen, the σ ⊥ of PEDOT:PSS/Te is 31.9 ± 4 S/cm, this value approximately matches with the values in Ref. [24]. However, this value increases dramatically to be 71.4 ± 3.7 S/cm just when inserting 1 wt% of NC into PEDOT:PSS/Te and this value of σ ⊥ still increases to be 240.5 ± 5.1 S/cm at 4 wt% of NC. The increase in the σ of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composites as the increase in NC amount can be explained by both carrier concentration and carrier's mobility. As known, the σ can be explained from the relation:
(1)
σ = n e μ

Where n refers to the carrier’s concentration, μ refers to the carrier’s mobility and e refers to the electronic charge. The measurements of n and μ against the amount of NC were introduced in Fig. 7 . As shown, the μ and n are increased dramatically with increasing the amount of NC and this agrees with the increase in σ. In fact, the decoration of NC on the Te surface facilitates the charge transfer complex between the PEDOT:PSS polymeric chains and the functionalized groups on NC which leads to an increase in the adsorption/desorption of the PEDOT:PSS chains to the surface of NC@Te [1,3,4]. As a result, a strong π-π conjugated interaction is produced between PEDOT:PSS chains and NC@Te create PEDOT:PSS chains strongly coated on the surface of NC@Te. This leads to an expansion in the PEDOT:PSS molecular conformation and a high order of chain packing through the growth of the adsorbed PEDOT:PSS chains on the NC@Te. This behavior leads to the ring twisting of PEDOT:PSS polymer which in turn reduces the π-π conjugated defects in the PEDOT:PSS backbone and rises the electron delocalization degree and hence reduces the barrier of the hopping carriers [73,74] therefore, the carrier mobilities in PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te increase. Also, the strong π-π interactions of NC@Te with the PEDOT:PSS chains leads to bridging the carrier transport, which increases the carrier mobility, thus, increasing the σ values. In fact, the presence of NC nano-dots on the surface of Te and strong adsorption of the segments of PEDOT:PSS on the NC@Te facilitate the creation of a conductive pathway of NC@Te inside the nano-composite template which facilitates the charge transfer channels by reducing the carrier jump barrier, therefore increasing the σ of the films [75,76].

XPS scan of typical S(2p) spectrum of the nano-composite film was carried out for confirming the above explanation (Fig. 8 ). As seen, Two spectra were detected at binding energies of 167–169 eV and 163–165 eV created from the sulphur atoms of sulfonate in PSS units and the sulphur atoms of thiophene of PEDOT units, respectively [1,22,28]. As seen, the effect of NC concentration has been clarified by the increase in the S(2p) intensity of the PEDOT and the decrease in the S(2p) intensity of the PSS. As observed the PEDOT:PSS/Te film has a ratio of PSS to PEDOT ~ 2.4 and for NC@Te/ PEDOT:PSS ~ 2.8, by using the ratio of the integral areas under the peaks related to PSS and PEDOT. As detected, there was a decrease in the S(2p) intensity of PSS and this spectrum shifted to a lower energy by the presence of NC. This behavior can be attributed to the strong π-π interaction of NC (via their functionalized groups) with the PEDOT:PSS polymer (via PSS chains). It is known that the PSS polymer exists in the PEDOT:PSS structure as a free and interacted with PEDOT. The interaction between NC and the insulating (free PSS and bonded PSS) leads to improving the carrier transport and facilitates the charge transfer channels by reducing the carrier jump barrier, hopping, and producing a higher σ value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Seebeck effect and power factor studies
ce:section.ce:para:
The values of S ⊥ and S // were measured with different amounts of NC nano-dots at room temperature (Fig. 9 ). As seen, the S // is almost dependent on NC content with a narrow range of variation. This behavior has been observed in Ref. [46]. On the other hand, the S ⊥ value of PEDOT:PSS/Te is 146.8 ± 2 μV/K, however, this value decreases to be 127.6 ± 3 μV/K just when inserting NC@Te (with 1 wt.% NC) into the polymer matrix and this value of S ⊥ decreased to be 78.8 ± 3 at 4 wt% of NC. In fact, when applying different temperatures on both terminates of the films, the electrons from the hotter terminate diffused to the colder terminate of the film due to the differences in the thermal velocity of the electrons between the two terminates. As a result, the electromotive forces are established between the film sides owing to the differences in the charge densities between the film sides. As shown, the positive values of S refer to the p-type semiconductor behavior of these nano-composites and the carrier's majorities are the holes [1,3,4]. In fact the decrease in the S value with increasing the NC content can be illustrated by the degenerate and non-degenerate semiconductors relations depending upon the Mott relation and the Boltzmann equations, Eqs. (2) and (3) [24,77]

where m* is the effective mass of the carrier, h is the Planck constant, r is a scatter parameter, and k B is the Boltzmann constant, respectively. From the relations, it is clear that S is inversely proportional to n. So, the decrease in the S values is ascribed to the growth in NC content, which in turns increases the (n). In fact, the growth in the values of (n) pushes the Fermi level Ef into the conduction band, lowers the work function (ϕ) of the nano-composite and changes the density of states (DOS) abruptly, leading to broadening and softening at the band edge; consequently, at Fermi levels (EF) the slope of the (DOS) decreases, which leads to a decline in S value according to Mott’s formula,

, in which EF = the Fermi level [1,3,4]. In fact, this matches the values of S for polymeric materials with amorphous or semi-crystalline nature, which are smaller than that of the semiconductors with crystalline nature [1].

UPS spectra were carried out for supporting the above results and explanations (Fig. 10 ). From the figure, the PEDOT:PSS/Te film has a work function (ϕ) larger than the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te film. From a UPS spectrum, the (ϕ) values of the films are determined by using the relation ϕ = hν – E0 – EF , where EF denotes to Fermi energy, E0 denotes to the secondary electron cut off and hν (21.22 eV) denotes to the source energy for (He I photons) source. The determined (ϕ) values for the NC@Te/PEDOT:PSS are found to be (4.32 eV) smaller than that of Te/PEDOT:PSS film (4.78 eV). The smaller values of (ϕ) indicate that the Fermi levels (EF) are pushing into the conduction band, where the DOS changes abruptly, as a result decreasing the value of S [1].

Alternatively, PF ⊥ and PF// values of the PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te nano-composites were figured with different NC content (Fig. 11 ). PF ⊥ and PF// values increased with the growth in the NC content. In fact, PF values of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te were determined according to the realtion
PF = σ

. Based on the carrier mobility this relation is transformed into

. Where μ refers to the carrier mobility, p refers to the carrier concentration of hole, e refers to the charge of electron and
N μ
refers to the effective density of states of transport levels. Accordingly, it can be observed that PF values are strongly dependant on μ . It was discovered that the increase in the content of NC nano-dots leads to an increase in μ and hence increasing the PF values. The increase in PF values is ascribed to the increase in the σ of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te. Where the growth in the σ of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te is ascribed to the strong interactions between PEDPT:PSS segments and their adjacent NC@Te nano-particles. These interactions lead to the formation of an NC@Te conductive path for facilitating the carrier transport. Indeed, the presence of a great number of interfaces inside the films such as interfaces between NC@Te and PEDOT:PSS chains creates a potential energy barrier that produces a carrier energy-filtering effect [1,3,4]. In fact, at these interfaces, the lower energy carriers are scattered and the carriers with high energy are passed and contributed in the transport mechanism, therefore PF values improved by the potential barrier filtering of nano-scales (Scheme 2 ). The increase in NC content leads to an increase the carrier’s mobility in these films, as a results, the increase in the carrier's mobility by the energy filtering and hence increasing the PF values of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te system [1,3,4].

The great enhancement in zT values of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te produced in this study was clearly observed in the analogy between this composite and other nano-composites based on PEDOT:PSS (Table 4 ). Our PEDOT:PS/NC@Te composite possess a higher zT values at a very small amount of fillers at R.T, which is considered one of the largest zT values in this regard as compared to the other earlier reported composites based on PEDOT:PSS. This highlights the role played by the NC nano-dots in enhancing σ, PF and controlling the values of k. The advantages and benefits were clearly shown for our thermoelectric materials such as using affordable, flexible, green and eco-friendly materials as well as using less raw materials, besides their high welfare, fast preparation, easy processability, their high mechanical and thermal stabilities when compared with previous thermoelectric materials. Great advantages of our composite also are represented in obtaining a high zT value at a tiny amount of NC content (3 wt%). On the other hand, a low S value was observed for our composite relative to the reported materials. In conclusion, the acid and/or base materials were recommended to manipulate/treat our composite which may enhance both S and σ, therefore improving the thermoelectric functions of these materials based on these nano-hybrid fillers.

Note: These results were obtained in January 2018. So we performed these measurements again in the present time (1 Nov 2020). Only the through plane measurements (σ ⊥, k ⊥ and S ⊥) were measured due to the in plane measurements are not available in our lab, which were performed by Prof. Farid El-Tantawy. The through plane measurements (σ ⊥ , k ⊥ and S ⊥) are figure with the NC content (see
Supplementary data ). It was found that the zT values reduced by 2.2 ± 0.1% from its original value. This reduction may be attributed to the air stability of the nanocomposite.

Nano-composite PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films have been engineered successfully through the casting approach for the thermoelectric applications for the first time. Decoration of NC nano-dots on the surface of Te nano-rods has been proven by several techniques such as XRD, TEM, and XPS. BET studies which confirmed that NC@Te nano-particles have a huge surface area, a typical IV isotherm and have meso and macro-pores on their surfaces which help in the adsorption of polymeric PEDOT:PSS chains and therefore decreasing the k values. The thermoelectric functions of PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te are greatly enhanced by the decoration of NC nano-dots on Te surface. The through plane σ ⊥ and in plane σ// values increased with increasing the NC nano-dots to be σ ⊥ = 165.3 ± 5 S/cm and σ// = 254.9 ± 10 S/cm at (3 wt% of NC) due to the creation of conductive paths inside the films and the increase in both carrier mobility and carrier concentration. Moreover, PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films show an under control through plane thermal conductivity k ⊥ behavior with a constant value (0.12 W/mK) due to (i) the creation of a gaseous atmosphere of nitrogen which occupies the defects and voids in the nano-composite; (ii) the large number of interfaces inside the nanocomposite between NC@Te and PEDOT:PSS leads to increasing the number of phonon scattering boundaries which in turn destroys the phonon transport and this is confirmed by the decrease in k p with increasing the NC content. The in plane thermal conductivity slightly increased with NC content thanks to the PSS chains orientation alignment to the film. The through plane S ⊥ value (95.3 ± 3 μV/K) was achieved at 3 wt% of NC nano-dot, due to the increase in the charge carriers and the reduction in work function. The through plane PF ⊥ was 154.2 ± 3 μV/mK2 and a through plane zT⊥ ~ 0.355 were achieved at a very small NC (3 wt%) content at room temperature. The in plane PF// shows higher values than the through plane as the σ// is higher than σ ⊥, while and in plane zT // shows a lower value than that in through plane since the k ⊥ is lower than k// . In conclusion, the obtained nano-composite PEDOT:PSS/NC@Te films have a great opportunity in many potential industrial applications in the field of catalysis and smart thermoelectric devices.
